Like I'll do, I'll even break out the speakers in a different place in many cases so that the actual pitch doesn't get interrupted. Now obviously there's people who are very, very effective, think boardroom, right, with long lists of bullets. But what's interesting is most of what you see sold that way has no personality or personal connection to the buyer. You are actually, you really are buying information. So boardroom, think book of inside information, right? So the letter's not really in anybody's voice, it's sort of a neutral, institutional voice. You're not hearing from Marty. You're not hearing how the inside information was gotten by traveling the world and talking to monks on top of the mountain and so forth. You're really buying the 400, the 12 foods they're ever to eat and what the IRS doesn't want you to know and all that kind of stuff. For me it gets in the way. You'll see me use a few through like a sales letter, but for me it gets in the way. Are you using like your stuff? Use long lists of bullets or no? Often. More than one way to skin the cat, but that's why I don't. Is for me it's like an interruption to the, yeah. Is there a difference in the way you use stories? Well, I'm sure there is, but what would be the difference in, say, your newsletter retention stuff versus a sales letter? Sales, length. Shorter sales letter. Yeah, I'll often find people tell a story like in a sales letter that goes on and on and on for like a page and a half and by the time they get to the end of it they don't remember where they started and what the, again it gets in the way of the rhythm and I'll force them to trim it. Whereas I might use that story full length with you because we have a relationship. We have the luxury of you being interested and fascinated in what's going on. So I can tell a longer story and be okay. And then purposing it. What is it supposed to do, obviously. Other than that, no, I don't think there's any difference. Good stories are good stories are good stories. So like all of the Rhone stories, which he now uses for teaching, those are all stories he used in MLM to recruit. So those are all really from opportunity meetings. It's where those are from. Those are all recruiting stories. Same with Turner. Every one of the Turner stories I'll give you comes from pitching people to pay 5,000 bucks to get in the business. Then later it gets used for other purposes. Good stories, good stories, good story. And as I showed you yesterday, same story can be used for five or six different purposes. What would have become apparent to me is that we have underappreciated the power of story. And the second thing is it's actually far more effective, more memorable, more, if you will, teaching, if you will, by telling the story than not. If I tell you, like here's a great Vance story. So like if you're going to tell people that if you force employees to do something without any coaching, without any rationale, without any psychology to it, you will get bad results. Now we can teach that until we're blue in the face and give them structures to do it and all that shit. The Vance story works infinitely better, which is the piss in the pool story. You guys all know the piss in the pool story? Trialing sales guy, they get a pool in the backyard. On the condition, because he's on the road all week, the little Johnny is going to take the net out every day and clean the pool and clean the filter. So first week he arrives home on Friday, says to his wife, how's the little Johnny doing? She says he hasn't touched a pool all damn week. He grabs the little Johnny by the scruff of the neck, takes him out to the pool, picks up the pool cleaner, throws Johnny in the pool, throws the pool cleaner in with him. Johnny cleans the pool. When he ain't looking, Johnny pisses in the pool. And so they will piss in your pool if you don't handle this exactly right. Vance then goes on to tell the story now differently where the punch line is. He throws the little Johnny in the pool, throws the pool cleaner in the pool, jumps in the pool with the little Johnny and helps him clean the pool. Different outcome. The story is infinitely better than all the diagrams and lists and teaching that you could possibly do. And as you say, it's memorable. So I'll have people, if I tell that to them and I always credit Vance, I will have people six, seven months later say, Harriet was pissing in the pool at the office and it's probably as much my fault as Harriet's because that sticks. So back to ideas that stick. So that's like a great visual. Vance is good at this too. See the nun on the bobsled story is a great story because I mean you ain't good or forget two nuns on a bobsled once he's told you the story. I mean you just, you know, right? And so yeah, it's infinitely more powerful. Easier to do the other by the way, you know, outlying five points yada yada. I figured that. You did. You have to write these actually. I've got to figure it out. As far as I'm concerned, you could have left yesterday. I mean you haven't been contributing at all just for the record. You're more of a nuisance. You know what it really comes down to is, I mean in a macro level, really, when I look at my own business, especially over the years, the main reason people have done business with me, you put aside property stuff, you put aside all that stuff is because of life. I mean that's really what it comes down to. And they like, see this loops back to another thing on the list, they like what you symbolically represent to them. And I think you symbolically represent to them a part of Americana, sort of Norman Rockwell, you know, Americana that's trustworthy. There is the diner where everybody goes and everybody knows you're, you know, see I think that's what you represent, right? I think symbolically ultimately for a lot of people what I wind up representing is being able to make things happen, live life on your own terms, make money from thin air, you know, that's like the symbolism for them. And so they like that too. And so I think that's, yeah, they like you and they like what you represent. All of the writing from an influence standpoint, the influence needs to be geared towards that. That's it. Well, I mean, yeah, and look, you know you don't sell numbers. Right. Well, I've never had anybody in 16, 17 years say they want a five part diagram of all this, you know, never. And it doesn't matter if it's a four unit property or a four hundred unit property. It doesn't matter. Well, that's right. See, well, that's right. Look, again, back to my point, see ultimately what they want to know is, is this guy real? If he is, then I can relieve myself of all that responsibility. Okay. Because I got a guy. Right. Because remember, people are, you know, cabbage thing. Everybody's walking around with the umbilical cord looking for the new place to plug it in. Right. People want to get rid of responsibility. They want to be told what to do. Preferably they want to have somebody do it for them. They want to be connected to somebody who solves a whole set of problems and needs and so forth for them. And so if they determine you're real and they resonate with you. So somebody who's lived, born and raised in Manhattan. Let's take Sydney. Okay. You would have zero appeal to Sydney Barrows providing investments because she has no connection to you in any way, shape or form. Born, raised in Manhattan, never left Manhattan. She don't even know where the hell Iowa is. She don't know who Andy Griffith is. She don't know, ain't got none of that. So to get a check out of her and keep her as investor would be a Herculean project. Right. And so obviously you're better off sacrificing her, not talking to her at all, not trying to. You don't care if she gets you. But the one who gets you, really gets you. And then they're like, I don't need to know the numbers. As Scott says, I don't even want to friggin' know the numbers. You know? That's fine. Here's a check. You know, send me pictures of the building and pictures of your dog and you know, send me some money once in a while.
